1:
On 1 Corinthians 15:1 — “Moreover, brethren, I declare unto you the gospel which I preached unto you, which also ye have received, and wherein ye stand” — the witnesses mark the opening of the great resurrection chapter.

The Eastern fathers note its weight. Chrysostom: having finished spiritual gifts, “he passes to that which is of all most necessary, the subject of the resurrection,” for in this they were “greatly unsound.” The grammatical-historical Barnes: this chapter is “more important than any other portion… an unanswerable argument for the main truth of Christianity.” Ellicott: occasioned by “the denial of the resurrection by some.” The Reformed Calvin: their belief had been “shaken by some wicked persons.”

The verse opens Paul’s sustained treatment of the resurrection. He reminds the Corinthians of the gospel he preached, which they received and in which they stand — the firm ground he will defend against those denying the resurrection of the dead.

So the witnesses see Paul begin the climactic argument of the epistle. The witnesses note that he starts not with abstract debate but with the gospel itself — the message he preached, they received, and on which they stand as on firm footing. They mark that the occasion was the denial of the resurrection by some at Corinth, perhaps under the influence of Greek philosophy that scorned bodily resurrection. They observe Chrysostom’s diagnosis that this was a sickness laying hold of a vital part, and Barnes’s judgment that the chapter is the great New Testament argument for the central fact of the faith. By recalling the gospel they already believed, Paul lays the foundation for his whole case: the resurrection of the dead stands or falls with the resurrection of Christ, which is itself part of the gospel they had received. The verse thus sets the stage — the gospel in which they stand — before Paul states, in the verses following, the very content of that gospel: Christ died, was buried, rose, and was seen.

So the witnesses see Paul begin the climactic argument of the epistle. The witnesses note that he starts not with abstract debate but with the gospel itself — the message he preached, they received, and on which they stand. They mark that the occasion was the denial of the resurrection by some at Corinth, perhaps under Greek philosophy that scorned bodily resurrection. By recalling the gospel they already believed, Paul lays the foundation for his whole case: the resurrection of the dead stands or falls with the resurrection of Christ, which is part of the gospel they had received.
2:
On 1 Corinthians 15:2 — “By which also ye are saved, if ye keep in memory what I preached unto you, unless ye have believed in vain” — the witnesses mark the saving gospel and the call to hold fast.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the conditions. Robertson: “in what words I preached it” — the message held fast. Ellicott: “if ye hold fast… unless you believed in vain” — a real, not idle, faith. Barnes: their salvation “depends” on this gospel. Wesley: “your salvation is begun, and will be perfected, if ye continue… unless ye have believed in vain.”

The verse states the saving power of the gospel and its condition. By it they are saved — if they hold fast what was preached, and have not believed in vain. Salvation is bound to perseverance in the true message.

So the witnesses see Paul press both the saving worth of the gospel and the necessity of holding it fast. The witnesses note that the gospel is the means of salvation — not a bare history but the power of God to save those who believe. Yet the saving is conditioned: “if ye keep in memory” or hold fast the message as preached. They mark Wesley’s sense that salvation is begun and will be perfected in those who continue in the faith. They observe the sobering clause “unless ye have believed in vain” — a faith that does not persevere, or that rests on a false report, proves empty and saves no one. This warning bears directly on the resurrection: to abandon the truth of Christ’s rising (which Paul will show is the heart of the gospel) would be to make their faith vain. The verse thus joins the gospel’s saving power to the duty of perseverance, and quietly forewarns that to deny the resurrection is to imperil the very faith by which they are saved. It prepares the recital of the gospel’s essential content.

So the witnesses see Paul press both the saving worth of the gospel and the necessity of holding it fast. The witnesses note that the gospel is the means of salvation — the power of God to save those who believe. Yet the saving is conditioned: hold fast the message as preached. Wesley’s sense is that salvation is begun and perfected in those who continue. The sobering “unless ye have believed in vain” warns that a faith that does not persevere proves empty — bearing directly on the resurrection, for to abandon the risen Christ would make their faith vain.
3:
On 1 Corinthians 15:3 — “For I delivered unto you first of all that which I also received, how that Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures” — the witnesses mark the first article of the gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the source and rank. Robertson: “first of all” (en protois), not in time but in importance; “which I also received” (parelabon) — direct revelation, as of the Supper (1 Corinthians 11:23). Clarke: “Christ died… as a vicarious sacrifice for sin” (Isaiah 53:5). The Reformed Calvin: the resurrection preached “as a fundamental doctrine.”

The verse states the gospel’s first and chief article: Christ died for our sins, as the Scriptures foretold. Paul delivered what he had himself received — the atoning death of Christ — as the foundation of all.

So the witnesses see Paul begin his summary of the gospel with the death of Christ. The witnesses note the weight of “first of all” — not merely first in sequence but first in importance, the fundamental matter. They mark that Paul “received” this gospel, by revelation from Christ himself, not as a human invention, and faithfully “delivered” it on. The first article is that “Christ died for our sins” — not a mere death, but a vicarious, atoning death, the just for the unjust, bearing the penalty of sin. And it was “according to the scriptures,” foretold in the prophets, especially in Isaiah’s suffering Servant, so that the cross fulfilled the ancient word. The verse thus lays the cornerstone of the gospel Paul preaches and will defend: the substitutionary death of Christ for sinners, witnessed beforehand by Scripture. It is the first of the four facts — died, buried, raised, seen — by which Paul defines the gospel, and the ground on which the doctrine of resurrection will rest.

So the witnesses see Paul begin his summary of the gospel with the death of Christ. The witnesses note the weight of “first of all” — first in importance, the fundamental matter. Paul “received” this by revelation from Christ himself, not as human invention, and faithfully delivered it. The first article is that “Christ died for our sins” — a vicarious, atoning death, the just for the unjust — and “according to the scriptures,” foretold in Isaiah’s suffering Servant. It is the cornerstone of the gospel: the substitutionary death of Christ, witnessed beforehand by Scripture.
4:
On 1 Corinthians 15:4 — “And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures” — the witnesses mark the burial and the resurrection.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the proofs. Robertson: each fact prefaced by “that” (hoti); the burial “an important detail”; “hath been raised” (egegertai, perfect — abiding result). Ellicott: the burial proves “the reality of His death.” Wesley: proved “from scripture” (Psalm 16:10). The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem: as with the cross, “verify the proofs of His Resurrection.”

The verse adds the second and third articles: Christ was buried, and rose again the third day, as the Scriptures foretold. The burial seals the reality of his death; the resurrection, foretold of old, is the heart of the gospel.

So the witnesses see Paul complete the gospel’s objective facts with the burial and the resurrection. The witnesses note the significance of the burial: it is no idle detail but the proof that Christ truly died — not a swoon or trance, but death sealed by the tomb. They mark the great central fact: “he rose again the third day,” expressed in the perfect tense (he “has been raised”), denoting not merely a past event but an abiding state — he rose and remains alive. And this too was “according to the scriptures,” foretold (Wesley notes Psalm 16) that God would not leave his Holy One in the grave. They observe Cyril of Jerusalem’s appeal to verify the resurrection by the Scriptures as the cross was verified. The verse thus sets the resurrection within the gospel itself, foretold and fulfilled: Christ died, was buried, and rose. These are the facts Paul preached and the Corinthians received; on the third, the resurrection, his whole argument will turn, for to deny the resurrection of the dead is to deny the risen Christ who is the gospel’s heart.

So the witnesses see Paul complete the gospel’s objective facts with the burial and the resurrection. The witnesses note the significance of the burial: no idle detail but the proof that Christ truly died, sealed by the tomb. They mark the central fact, “he rose again the third day,” in the perfect tense (he “has been raised”) — not merely a past event but an abiding state. And this too was “according to the scriptures,” foretold (Ps 16) that God would not leave his Holy One in the grave. On this third fact Paul’s whole argument will turn.
5:
On 1 Corinthians 15:5 — “And that he was seen of Cephas, then of the twelve” — the witnesses mark the first appearances.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the witnesses. Robertson: “he appeared” (ophthe) — “not a mere vision, but actual appearance.” Ellicott: arranged chronologically — to Cephas (Luke 24:34), then the Twelve. Clarke notes “the twelve” as the standing title though the full number was not present. The Reformed Calvin: he brings forward the “eye-witnesses” who saw the fulfilment.

The verse begins the list of resurrection witnesses. Christ was seen by Cephas (Peter), then by the Twelve. The risen Lord appeared bodily to chosen witnesses, whose testimony establishes the fact.

So the witnesses see Paul move from the foretold facts to the proof by eyewitnesses. The witnesses note that the resurrection, like any fact, is established by competent testimony, and Paul marshals a series of appearances. The first named is to Cephas — Peter — singled out, perhaps as a special grace to the one who had denied his Lord; then to “the Twelve,” the standing title of the apostolic college though their full number was not always present. They mark the force of the verb “was seen”: not a subjective vision or dream, but the actual appearance of the risen Christ, seen with the eyes. They observe Calvin’s point that these are the eye-witnesses who saw accomplished what Scripture had foretold. The verse thus begins the irrefutable testimony: the risen Lord was seen, first by Peter and the apostles, men who knew him and could not be deceived. Against the deniers at Corinth, Paul sets the weight of living witnesses, building toward the overwhelming proof of the five hundred and his own encounter — the resurrection attested not by argument alone but by those who saw.

So the witnesses see Paul move from the foretold facts to the proof by eyewitnesses. The witnesses note that the first named is to Cephas — Peter — singled out, perhaps as a special grace to the one who had denied his Lord; then to “the Twelve,” the standing title of the apostolic college. They mark the force of “was seen”: not a subjective vision but the actual appearance of the risen Christ, seen with the eyes. Against the deniers, Paul sets the weight of living witnesses who knew the Lord and could not be deceived.
6:
On 1 Corinthians 15:6 — “After that, he was seen of above five hundred brethren at once; of whom the greater part remain unto this present, but some are fallen asleep” — the witnesses mark the appearance to the five hundred.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the force of the testimony. Robertson: probably the meeting on the Galilean mountain (Matthew 28:16); “the greater part remain” — the witnesses could be questioned. Clarke: “five hundred persons saw him at one time… a remarkable testimony.” Ellicott: “fallen asleep” (cf. Acts 7:60), the Christian word for death.

The verse gives the most public proof: over five hundred saw the risen Christ at once, most of them still living when Paul wrote. The testimony could be tested by questioning the surviving witnesses.

So the witnesses see Paul press the overwhelming weight of the five hundred. The witnesses note the force of the number: not a private vision to one or two, but more than five hundred brethren seeing the risen Lord at a single time — likely the prearranged meeting in Galilee. They mark the pointed remark that “the greater part remain unto this present”: Paul effectively invites the doubters to inquire of the living witnesses, who could confirm the fact. Such a multitude could not be deceived together, nor could the testimony be a conspiracy. They observe the tender word for the few who had died — “fallen asleep,” the Christian term that itself implies awakening, fitting in a chapter on resurrection. The verse thus brings forward the strongest historical evidence: a large body of eye-witnesses, mostly alive and available for cross-examination. Against the skeptics at Corinth, Paul stakes the resurrection on verifiable testimony — not legend, but the report of hundreds who saw, a proof that demanded to be reckoned with.

So the witnesses see Paul press the overwhelming weight of the five hundred. The witnesses note the force of the number: not a private vision to one or two, but more than five hundred brethren seeing the risen Lord at a single time, likely the prearranged meeting in Galilee. They mark the pointed remark that “the greater part remain”: Paul effectively invites the doubters to inquire of the living witnesses. Such a multitude could not be deceived together. The tender word for the few who had died — “fallen asleep” — itself implies awakening, fitting in a chapter on resurrection.
7:
On 1 Corinthians 15:7 — “After that, he was seen of James; then of all the apostles” — the witnesses mark the further appearances.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the witnesses. Robertson: “to James” the brother of the Lord — explaining the brothers’ presence in the upper room (Acts 1:14); “to all the apostles” at the Ascension. Barnes: this appearance to James is not in the Gospels, the Lord appearing often in the forty days. Wesley takes “all the apostles” to include the seventy.

The verse adds two more appearances: to James, and then to all the apostles. The risen Christ was seen by his own brother and by the whole apostolic company before his ascension.

So the witnesses see Paul continue the chain of testimony with James and all the apostles. The witnesses note the appearance to James — understood by most to be the Lord’s brother, later the leader of the Jerusalem church — an appearance not recorded in the Gospels but known to the early church, and one that may explain how the once-unbelieving brothers of Jesus came to be gathered with the disciples after the resurrection. They mark “then of all the apostles,” a final appearance to the whole company, likely at the Ascension on Olivet. They observe that the Lord appeared many times during the forty days, so that not all the appearances are recorded; Paul selects representative ones. The verse thus extends the testimony across a range of witnesses — an individual (James), and the full apostolic body — further establishing the breadth and certainty of the proof. The risen Christ was seen not once but repeatedly, by many and by various, before Paul comes to the most personal witness of all: himself, the last and least.

So the witnesses see Paul continue the chain of testimony with James and all the apostles. The witnesses note the appearance to James — understood by most to be the Lord’s brother, later the Jerusalem leader — not recorded in the Gospels but known to the early church, and one that may explain how the once-unbelieving brothers came to be gathered with the disciples. They mark “then of all the apostles,” likely at the Ascension. The Lord appeared many times in the forty days; Paul selects representative appearances, extending the breadth and certainty of the proof.
8:
On 1 Corinthians 15:8 — “And last of all he was seen of me also, as of one born out of due time” — the witnesses mark Paul’s own witness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the strange word. Robertson: “as to the miscarriage” (ektromati), an untimely birth (LXX Numbers 12:12; Job 3:16). Barnes: Paul saw the same risen Lord as the others (Acts 9:5), else his witness proved nothing. Wesley: “as an abortion is not worthy the name of a man, so he… not worthy the name of an apostle.”

The verse gives the last appearance — to Paul himself, “as to one born out of due time.” The risen Christ appeared to him at his conversion, and Paul, with deep humility, likens himself to an untimely birth.

So the witnesses see Paul add himself, last of all, to the roll of witnesses. The witnesses note the importance of his claim: he saw the same risen Lord, in the same bodily reality, as Peter and the rest — for unless his vision on the Damascus road was an appearance of the living Christ, his testimony would prove nothing of the resurrection. They mark the striking self-description, “one born out of due time” — the word means a miscarriage or untimely birth. The expositors take it to express both the suddenness and violence of his irregular “birth” into apostleship (called last, out of the normal course) and, above all, his deep humility: as an abortion is unworthy the name of a man, so he counts himself scarcely worthy the name of apostle. They observe the touching pathos in the phrase. The verse thus completes the witnesses with Paul’s own encounter, joining his testimony to the others while abasing himself before the grace that called him. It leads naturally into his confession of unworthiness and his magnifying of grace in the verses that follow.

So the witnesses see Paul add himself, last of all, to the roll of witnesses. The witnesses note the importance of his claim: he saw the same risen Lord, in the same bodily reality, as Peter and the rest — for unless his Damascus vision was an appearance of the living Christ, his testimony would prove nothing. They mark the striking self-description, “one born out of due time” — a miscarriage or untimely birth — expressing both the suddenness of his irregular call and, above all, his deep humility: scarcely worthy the name of apostle. It leads into his confession of unworthiness and his magnifying of grace.
9:
On 1 Corinthians 15:9 — “For I am the least of the apostles, that am not meet to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the church of God” — the witnesses mark Paul’s confession of unworthiness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the humility. Robertson: “the least” (true superlative), cf. “less than the least of all saints” (Ephesians 3:8), “chief” of sinners (1 Timothy 1:15). Barnes: not from defect of commission but “the great crime of his life” — persecuting the church. Wesley: “true believers are humbled all their lives, even for the sins… before they believed.”

The verse gives Paul’s reason for the abasing word. He is the least of the apostles, unworthy of the name, because he persecuted the church of God. The memory of his past keeps him low.

So the witnesses see Paul ground his self-abasement in his former persecution of the church. The witnesses note that he calls himself “the least of the apostles” — not from any defect in his apostolic commission or qualification, which he elsewhere defends stoutly, but from the abiding memory of his great sin in persecuting the believers. They mark the depth of his humility, of a piece with his calling himself “less than the least of all saints” and the “chief of sinners” — not false modesty but a true sense of what he had been. They observe Wesley’s point that true believers are humbled all their lives, even for sins committed before conversion, the remembrance of past guilt keeping them lowly. The verse thus shows the genuine humility of the apostle: though he had laboured and suffered more than all, he never forgot the crime from which grace had rescued him. This self-abasement is not the last word, however, but the dark background against which he will magnify the grace of God — for it is grace alone that made the persecutor an apostle, as the next verse declares.

So the witnesses see Paul ground his self-abasement in his former persecution of the church. The witnesses note that he calls himself “the least of the apostles” — not from any defect in his commission, which he elsewhere defends, but from the abiding memory of his great sin. They mark the depth of his humility, of a piece with “less than the least of all saints” and the “chief of sinners” — not false modesty but a true sense of what he had been. This self-abasement is the dark background against which he will magnify the grace that made the persecutor an apostle.
10:
On 1 Corinthians 15:10 — “But by the grace of God I am what I am: and his grace which was bestowed upon me was not in vain; but I laboured more abundantly than they all: yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me” — the witnesses mark the magnifying of grace.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the balance. Robertson: “what I am” (neuter) — his attainments, all “by the grace of God”; “I laboured more abundantly” is “sober fact.” The Reformed Calvin warns against those who “set free-will in opposition to… grace” — Paul ascribes all to God. Wesley: grace “qualified me… and still excites me to zeal… yet… not I, but the grace of God.”

The verse turns from confession to the praise of grace. Paul is what he is by God’s grace; that grace was not fruitless, for he laboured more than all — yet not he, but the grace of God with him. All is ascribed to grace.

So the witnesses see Paul magnify the grace that transformed the persecutor into the foremost labourer. The witnesses note the threefold movement: he is what he is — an apostle, a saint — wholly “by the grace of God,” not by native goodness or merit; that grace was “not in vain,” not idle, but bore abundant fruit; and he “laboured more abundantly than they all,” a plain fact attested by his ministry and sufferings. Yet at once he checks any boast: “not I, but the grace of God which was with me.” They mark Calvin’s insistence that this overthrows every notion of free-will set against grace — Paul ascribes both the working and the willing to God. They observe the perfect balance: grace neither idle (it produced great labour) nor self-produced (the labour was God’s grace at work). The verse thus crowns the digression on his unworthiness with the triumph of grace: the chief of sinners became the chief of labourers, and all the glory is God’s. It models the Christian’s true estimate of self — nothing in himself, everything by grace — before Paul returns to the common testimony of all the apostles.
11:
On 1 Corinthians 15:11 — “Therefore whether it were I or they, so we preach, and so ye believed” — the witnesses mark the united testimony.

The Eastern fathers note the equality. Chrysostom: having abased and then exalted himself to make out “an equality” with the apostles, he establishes his credit. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “so we preach, and so ye believed” — “this is Paul’s gospel,” what matters for preacher and hearer. The Reformed Calvin: “we have all taught so with one mouth, and still… teach so.”

The verse closes the witness-list with the unity of the apostolic message. Whether Paul or the others preached, they all proclaimed the same gospel — and so the Corinthians believed. The testimony is one.

So the witnesses see Paul gather up the foregoing into the single, united apostolic proclamation. The witnesses note that, having distinguished himself from and then equalled the other apostles, he now associates himself with them in perfect agreement: it matters not who preached, for all — greatest and least — proclaimed the same gospel, the death and resurrection of Christ. They mark that this unity is itself a proof: there was no rival message, no competing account; with one mouth all the apostles taught the risen Christ. And on this one gospel the Corinthians’ faith rested: “so ye believed.” They observe that this brings the argument back from Paul’s personal digression to the main point — the resurrection of Christ, attested by all the witnesses and preached by all the apostles, is the foundation of the faith the Corinthians had received. The verse thus seals the historical proof: the resurrection is no private opinion but the united testimony of all who saw and all who preached, the very thing the Corinthians believed — making their flirtation with denial the more inconsistent, as Paul will now press.

So the witnesses see Paul gather up the foregoing into the single, united apostolic proclamation. The witnesses note that, having distinguished himself from and then equalled the other apostles, he now associates himself with them in perfect agreement: it matters not who preached, for all — greatest and least — proclaimed the same risen Christ. This unity is itself a proof: there was no rival message, no competing account. And on this one gospel the Corinthians’ faith rested: “so ye believed” — making their flirtation with denial the more inconsistent, as Paul will now press.
12:
On 1 Corinthians 15:12 — “Now if Christ be preached that he rose from the dead, how say some among you that there is no resurrection of the dead?” — the witnesses mark the turn to the argument.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the logic. Robertson: the question springs from the proof of Christ’s resurrection (vv. 1–11). Ellicott: “it is… illogical to say there is no resurrection” when a man has risen. The Puritan Henry: some “flatly denied” it. The Reformed Calvin: he proves the resurrection of all from that of Christ, by “a mutual and reciprocal inference.” Wesley: the deniers “probably had been heathen philosophers.”

The verse turns from proof to argument. If Christ is preached as risen — as he is, and the Corinthians believe — how can some among them say there is no resurrection of the dead? The contradiction is exposed.

So the witnesses see Paul confront the deniers with their own inconsistency. The witnesses note the pivot: having established the fact of Christ’s resurrection by many witnesses, Paul now uses it to overthrow the denial of the resurrection of the dead. The contradiction is plain: a man — Christ — has demonstrably risen; how then can it be held that there is no such thing as resurrection? They mark Calvin’s observation of the reciprocal inference Paul will press: from Christ’s rising to ours, and from the impossibility of ours back to the denial of his. They observe that the deniers, probably influenced by Greek philosophy that scorned bodily resurrection, had not reckoned with the consequence — that to deny resurrection in general is to deny Christ’s, the very gospel they professed. The verse thus opens the great chain of consequences Paul will unfold: he will show, step by step, the ruinous absurdities that follow from denying the resurrection, driving the Corinthians back from their error by exposing where it logically leads. The risen Christ makes the denial untenable.

So the witnesses see Paul confront the deniers with their own inconsistency. The witnesses note the pivot: having established the fact of Christ’s resurrection by many witnesses, Paul uses it to overthrow the denial of the resurrection of the dead. The contradiction is plain: a man — Christ — has demonstrably risen; how then can it be held that there is no such thing as resurrection? They mark Calvin’s reciprocal inference: from Christ’s rising to ours, and from the impossibility of ours back to the denial of his. The risen Christ makes the denial untenable.
13:
On 1 Corinthians 15:13 — “But if there be no resurrection of the dead, then is Christ not risen” — the witnesses mark the first consequence.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the force. Robertson: “he turns the argument round with tremendous force. But it is fair.” Barnes: the same difficulties existed in raising Christ as in any case; deny the general, and you deny the particular. Adam Clarke: Christ shared our flesh, so if the dead rise not, “Christ has had no resurrection.”

The verse draws the first inference. If there is no resurrection of the dead at all, then Christ himself is not risen. The denial of the general principle destroys the particular fact.

So the witnesses see Paul begin the chain of consequences with a devastating logical stroke. The witnesses note the fairness and force of the move: if it is held, as a general principle, that the dead do not rise — that resurrection is impossible — then Christ, who was truly dead and buried, cannot have risen either, for the same impossibility would bar his rising as any other’s. They mark Clarke’s point that Christ shared our very flesh and blood, so his resurrection stands or falls with the possibility of resurrection in general. The deniers cannot exempt Christ from their own rule. They observe that Paul thus binds the fate of Christ’s resurrection to the doctrine the Corinthians were tempted to abandon: to deny the one is to deny the other. The verse opens the series of ruinous conclusions: by denying the resurrection of the dead, the Corinthians unwittingly deny the risen Christ — and with him, as the following verses show, the whole gospel, their faith, their forgiveness, and their hope. The argument from absurdity has begun.

The witnesses note the fairness and force: if it is held, as a general principle, that the dead do not rise, then Christ, who was truly dead and buried, cannot have risen either, for the same impossibility would bar his rising. They mark Clarke’s point that Christ shared our very flesh, so his resurrection stands or falls with the possibility of resurrection in general. The deniers cannot exempt Christ from their own rule — the argument from absurdity has begun.
14:
On 1 Corinthians 15:14 — “And if Christ be not risen, then is our preaching vain, and your faith is also vain” — the witnesses mark the emptiness that would follow.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word. Robertson: “vain” (kenon), empty — both preaching and faith “empty if Christ has not been raised.” Barnes: it would be useless to preach, for the substance was a risen Christ. The Reformed Calvin: “not simply… some mixture of falsehood, but… altogether an empty fallacy” — for what remains if Christ is “swallowed up by death”?

The verse states the next consequence. If Christ is not risen, the apostles’ preaching is empty and the Corinthians’ faith is empty too. The whole gospel collapses into hollow falsehood.

So the witnesses see Paul press the consequence to the heart of the gospel. The witnesses note that the resurrection is no detachable appendix but the very substance of the apostolic message: if Christ is not risen, then the preaching that proclaimed a risen Christ is “vain” — empty, hollow, void of content and of power. And the faith that received that preaching is equally empty, resting on nothing. They mark Calvin’s strong sense: not merely a gospel with some admixture of error, but an utter, empty fallacy, for if Christ has been swallowed up by death there is nothing left to believe or preach. They observe that Paul stakes everything on the resurrection: remove it, and both the message and the believing are reduced to nothing. The verse thus shows the deniers what their position would cost — not a minor adjustment of doctrine, but the evacuation of the entire gospel and of their own faith. It is the second link in the chain of absurdities: deny the resurrection, and preaching and faith alike are emptied, leaving the Corinthians with nothing at all.

So the witnesses see Paul press the consequence to the heart of the gospel. The witnesses note that the resurrection is no detachable appendix but the very substance of the message: if Christ is not risen, then the preaching that proclaimed a risen Christ is “vain” — empty, void of content and power. And the faith that received it is equally empty, resting on nothing. They mark Calvin’s strong sense: not a gospel with some admixture of error, but an utter, empty fallacy, for if Christ has been swallowed up by death there is nothing left to believe or preach.
15:
On 1 Corinthians 15:15 — “Yea, and we are found false witnesses of God; because we have testified of God that he raised up Christ: whom he raised not up, if so be that the dead rise not” — the witnesses mark the slander against God.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the gravity. Robertson: “false witnesses of God” (pseudomartyres) — the genitive both “in God’s service” and “concerning God.” Ellicott: “not mistaken witnesses, but… deceivers.” The Reformed Calvin: a grave absurdity — that the apostles, ordained to witness, should be liars about God himself.

The verse adds a further consequence. If the dead rise not, the apostles are exposed as false witnesses of God, for they testified that he raised Christ — whom, on that supposition, he did not raise. They would be liars about God.

So the witnesses see Paul press the consequence to its most shocking point: the apostles would be liars against God. The witnesses note the weight of the charge that would fall on the apostles: they had borne solemn witness that God raised Christ from the dead; but if there is no resurrection, then God did no such thing, and they are convicted of testifying falsely — not as honestly mistaken men, but, as Ellicott stresses, as deliberate deceivers, fabricating a lie and attributing it to God himself. They mark the double force of “false witnesses of God”: witnesses in God’s service who falsified, and witnesses who lied concerning God’s own act. They observe Calvin’s point that this is no trivial absurdity — the very men ordained to declare God’s truth would be blaspheming him by a fabricated resurrection. The verse thus deepens the chain: deny the resurrection, and you must brand the apostles, and all their testimony, as a lie told against God. The deniers cannot escape the conclusion that their position makes God’s appointed witnesses into impostors — an absurdity Paul will compound in the verses that follow.

So the witnesses see Paul press the consequence to its most shocking point: the apostles would be liars against God. The witnesses note the weight of the charge: they had borne solemn witness that God raised Christ; but if there is no resurrection, then God did no such thing, and they are convicted of testifying falsely — not as honestly mistaken men, but, as Ellicott stresses, as deliberate deceivers, fabricating a lie and attributing it to God. They mark Calvin’s point that this is no trivial absurdity — the very men ordained to declare God’s truth would be blaspheming him.
16:
On 1 Corinthians 15:16 — “For if the dead rise not, then is not Christ raised” — the witnesses mark the repeated premise.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the repetition. Robertson: “repeats the position already taken in verse 13.” Barnes: repeated “because of its importance… a great and momentous truth.” Ellicott: the denial of the doctrine cannot be maintained unless Christ’s resurrection itself is overthrown. Gill: made “to show the importance of it.”

The verse repeats the pivotal premise. If the dead do not rise, then Christ is not raised. Paul restates the linchpin of his argument before drawing out its further consequences.

So the witnesses see Paul deliberately repeat the crucial inference of verse 13. The witnesses note that the repetition is not redundant but emphatic: the truth is so weighty, and so decisive for the whole argument, that Paul drives it home a second time. The denial of resurrection in general necessarily carries with it the denial of Christ’s resurrection in particular — the two cannot be separated. They mark Barnes’s observation that a great and momentous truth bears repeating. They observe that by restating the premise, Paul prepares to unfold a further and more personal set of consequences: not only is preaching vain and the apostles liars (vv. 14–15), but, as the next verse will show, the Corinthians’ own faith is futile and they remain in their sins. The verse thus serves as the hinge on which the second half of the argument turns — restating that the fate of Christ’s resurrection is bound to the doctrine the Corinthians were tempted to deny, so that the ruinous conclusions about to be drawn follow inexorably from their error.

So the witnesses see Paul deliberately repeat the crucial inference of verse 13. The witnesses note that the repetition is not redundant but emphatic: the truth is so weighty, and so decisive for the whole argument, that Paul drives it home a second time. The denial of resurrection in general necessarily carries with it the denial of Christ’s in particular. They observe that by restating the premise, Paul prepares to unfold a further and more personal set of consequences — not only is preaching vain and the apostles liars, but the Corinthians’ own faith is futile and they remain in their sins.
17:
On 1 Corinthians 15:17 — “And if Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins” — the witnesses mark the loss of forgiveness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the stronger word. Robertson: “vain” (mataia, devoid of truth) — stronger than kenon (v. 14); “yet in your sins,” for the death of Christ has no atoning value if he did not rise. Wesley: needed “something more than reformation… even that atonement” attested by the resurrection. The Reformed Calvin connects atonement and the conquest of death (Romans 6:6).

The verse names the gravest personal consequence. If Christ is not raised, their faith is futile and they are still in their sins. Without the resurrection, there is no forgiveness.

So the witnesses see Paul press the consequence home to the conscience: no risen Christ, no pardon. The witnesses note the stronger word for “vain” here — not merely empty but false, a delusion — for a faith resting on a Christ still dead is a lie. They mark the dreadful inference: “ye are yet in your sins.” The resurrection is God’s seal upon the atonement; if Christ remained in the grave, his death accomplished no expiation, was not accepted, and the believer’s guilt remains unremoved. They observe Wesley’s point that more than reformation was needed — an atonement, whose sufficiency God attested by raising the Surety from the dead; and Calvin’s, that the conquest of sin and death stands with the resurrection. The verse thus shows the deniers that their position strips them of the very forgiveness they sought: a dead Christ saves no one, and they would still bear their sins. It is the most piercing link in the chain — the resurrection is not a remote doctrine but the ground of pardon; deny it, and the soul is left unforgiven, its faith a hollow deception.

So the witnesses see Paul press the consequence home to the conscience: no risen Christ, no pardon. The witnesses note the stronger word for “vain” here — not merely empty but false, a delusion. They mark the dreadful inference: “ye are yet in your sins.” The resurrection is God’s seal upon the atonement; if Christ remained in the grave, his death accomplished no accepted expiation, and the guilt remains. Wesley notes more than reformation was needed — an atonement, attested by raising the Surety. It is the most piercing link: deny the resurrection, and the soul is left unforgiven.
18:
On 1 Corinthians 15:18 — “Then they also which are fallen asleep in Christ are perished” — the witnesses mark the ruin of the dead believers.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the force. Robertson: “have perished” (apolonto, second aorist) — “delivered up to eternal misery.” Barnes: those who died as Christians “failed of salvation.” Clarke: “their hope was without foundation.” Wesley: those who “sleep in Christ… have lost their life and being together.”

The verse extends the loss to the Christian dead. If Christ is not raised, then those who have died in Christ have perished. Their faith and hope would have ended in destruction.

So the witnesses see Paul carry the consequence to the believing dead. The witnesses note the tender phrase “fallen asleep in Christ” — the Christian way of speaking of death, which itself implies awakening; yet on the deniers’ premise, even these have simply “perished,” been destroyed, their hope proved baseless. They mark the gravity of the word: not merely that their bodies decay, but, as Robertson notes, that they would be lost, their faith having availed nothing. They observe the poignancy: the very believers who died trusting in Christ — some, perhaps, martyrs — would, if there is no resurrection, have gone to destruction, their confidence a delusion. The verse thus adds another grievous consequence: deny the resurrection, and the Christian dead are not asleep awaiting glory but perished without hope. It strikes at the comfort the Corinthians had in their departed brethren, showing that the denial robs not only the living of pardon but the dead of salvation. The horror of this conclusion presses toward the climax of the next verse — that Christians would be the most pitiable of all.

So the witnesses see Paul carry the consequence to the believing dead. The witnesses note the tender phrase “fallen asleep in Christ,” which itself implies awakening; yet on the deniers’ premise, even these have simply “perished,” their hope proved baseless. They mark the gravity of the word: not merely that their bodies decay, but, as Robertson notes, that they would be lost. The very believers who died trusting in Christ — some perhaps martyrs — would have gone to destruction. The denial robs not only the living of pardon but the dead of salvation, pressing toward the climax of the next verse.
19:
On 1 Corinthians 15:19 — “If in this life only we have hope in Christ, we are of all men most miserable” — the witnesses mark the climax of the consequences.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the pathos. Robertson: “most pitiable” (eleeinoteroi, comparative) — hope “limited to this life even if ‘in Christ.’” Adam Clarke renders it: if “we have hoped in Christ only” in this life. The Reformed Calvin: it were “better for us not to believe,” the condition of unbelievers preferable. Wesley contrasts the hope “full of immortality.”

The verse reaches the climax of the dark consequences. If our hope in Christ is for this life only, we are the most pitiable of all men. Without resurrection, the Christian’s lot is the saddest of all.

So the witnesses see Paul bring the chain of absurdities to its most affecting point. The witnesses note the supposition: if our hope in Christ reaches no further than this present life — if there is no resurrection and no world to come — then Christians, of all people, are the most to be pitied. They have forgone the world’s pleasures, borne reproach, suffered persecution, and staked everything on a future that, on this supposition, does not exist. They mark Calvin’s stark conclusion: in that case it were better not to have believed at all, the unbeliever’s condition being preferable. They observe Wesley’s contrast — but the Christian does have a hope full of immortality, a taste of the powers of the world to come. The verse thus completes the negative argument with its most poignant absurdity: to deny the resurrection is to make the believing, suffering Christian the most wretched of men, having given up the present for a non-existent future. The very intolerableness of this conclusion prepares the glorious turn of the next verse — “but now is Christ risen” — where Paul casts off the hypothesis and proclaims the triumphant fact.

So the witnesses see Paul bring the chain of absurdities to its most affecting point. The witnesses note the supposition: if our hope in Christ reaches no further than this present life, then Christians, of all people, are the most to be pitied. They have forgone the world’s pleasures, borne reproach and persecution, and staked everything on a future that, on this view, does not exist. They mark Calvin’s stark conclusion: in that case it were better not to have believed at all. The very intolerableness of this prepares the glorious turn — “but now is Christ risen.”
20:
On 1 Corinthians 15:20 — “But now is Christ risen from the dead, and become the firstfruits of them that slept” — the witnesses mark the triumphant turn.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the burst of certainty. Robertson: “but now” (nyni de), “the logical triumph of Paul”; “firstfruits” (aparche). Lightfoot ties it to the offering of the sheaf. The Puritan Henry: Christ “the first-fruits of those that slept… in this very quality.” Barnes: “the bursting forth of a full heart and overpowering conviction.”

The verse turns from the dark hypothesis to the glorious fact. But now Christ is risen, the firstfruits of those who have died. His resurrection is the pledge and beginning of theirs.

So the witnesses see Paul cast off the gloomy supposition with a shout of certainty. The witnesses note the emphatic “but now” — the logical and emotional triumph after the piled-up absurdities: it is not so; Christ is risen indeed. They mark the rich figure “firstfruits” — drawn from the sheaf offered to God as the pledge of the coming harvest. As the firstfruits guarantee and represent the whole crop, so the risen Christ is the pledge, the sample, and the beginning of the resurrection of all his people; his rising ensures theirs. They observe that this overturns every dark consequence: because Christ is risen, preaching is not vain, faith is not empty, sins are forgiven, the dead in Christ are not perished, and the Christian’s hope is not confined to this life. The verse thus marks the pivot of the chapter, from the negative argument to the positive proclamation: the resurrection of Christ is a fact, and that fact is the firstfruits guaranteeing the harvest of the dead. Paul will now unfold how, in Christ the second Adam, that harvest comes.

So the witnesses see Paul cast off the gloomy supposition with a shout of certainty. The witnesses note the emphatic “but now” — the logical and emotional triumph after the piled-up absurdities: it is not so; Christ is risen indeed. They mark the rich figure “firstfruits” — the sheaf offered to God as the pledge of the coming harvest. As the firstfruits guarantee the whole crop, so the risen Christ is the pledge and beginning of the resurrection of all his people; his rising ensures theirs. This overturns every dark consequence and marks the pivot of the chapter.
21:
On 1 Corinthians 15:21 — “For since by man came death, by man came also the resurrection of the dead” — the witnesses mark the two representative men.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the parallel. Robertson: “by man also” — Jesus, the God-man, the Second Adam (Romans 5:12). Barnes: by Adam’s transgression all are subject to death. Adam Clarke: “mortality came by Adam, immortality by Christ.” The Reformed Calvin: death is “not from nature, but from man’s sin”; as Adam died not for himself alone, so Christ.

The verse sets the two heads of humanity in parallel. As death came by a man (Adam), so the resurrection comes by a man (Christ). The remedy answers the ruin, head for head.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the resurrection in the parallel of the two representative men. The witnesses note the symmetry: death entered the human race through one man, Adam, whose sin brought mortality on all his posterity; so the resurrection of the dead comes through one man, Christ, the second Adam, whose rising brings life to all who are his. They mark Calvin’s point that death is not natural but the fruit of sin, and that as Adam acted not for himself alone but as the head of the race, so Christ acts as the new head, his resurrection reaching to those he represents. They observe Clarke’s neat antithesis — mortality by Adam, immortality by Christ. The verse thus explains how the firstfruits guarantee the harvest: by the principle of representation. Just as humanity fell in its first head and died, so it rises in its new head and lives. The resurrection is not an isolated wonder but the working-out of Christ’s headship over a new humanity — a truth Paul will state even more sharply in the next verse’s “in Adam… in Christ.”

So the witnesses see Paul ground the resurrection in the parallel of the two representative men. The witnesses note the symmetry: death entered through one man, Adam, whose sin brought mortality on all his posterity; so the resurrection comes through one man, Christ, the second Adam, whose rising brings life to all who are his. They mark Calvin’s point that death is not natural but the fruit of sin, and that as Adam acted as head of the race, so Christ acts as the new head. The resurrection is the working-out of Christ’s headship over a new humanity.
22:
On 1 Corinthians 15:22 — “For as in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive” — the witnesses mark the two solidarities.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the scope of “all.” Robertson: “made alive” (zoopoiethesontai) — “not easy to catch Paul’s thought” on the second “all.” Ellicott: “in the Adam… in the Christ,” the heads of humanity. Wesley: not “shall revive” but “shall be made alive, by a power not their own” — the “all” in Christ being “these,” the righteous.

The verse states the great parallel of solidarity. As all who are in Adam die, so all who are in Christ shall be made alive. Each head conveys his condition to those united to him.

So the witnesses see Paul set the two great solidarities side by side. The witnesses note the principle of union: all who are “in Adam” — the whole race descended from him — share his death; and all who are “in Christ” — united to him by faith — shall share his life, being “made alive” by a power not their own. They mark the debate over the second “all”: most of the expositors, with Wesley, hold that “in Christ shall all be made alive” refers to the blessed resurrection of believers, the “all” being those who are in Christ, not every person without exception — for the chapter’s theme is the glorious raising of the dead in Christ, and Robertson notes the difficulty of pressing the parallel to universal salvation. They observe Calvin’s and others’ care that the two “alls” are each defined by their head. The verse thus crowns the Adam-Christ parallel: as death reigns over all in the first head, so life-giving resurrection comes to all in the second — the firstfruits drawing the harvest of his own. It grounds the believer’s hope in union with the risen Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul set the two great solidarities side by side. The witnesses note the principle of union: all who are “in Adam” share his death; all who are “in Christ,” united by faith, shall share his life, “made alive” by a power not their own. They mark the debate over the second “all”: most, with Wesley, hold that “in Christ shall all be made alive” means the blessed resurrection of believers, the “all” being those in Christ, not every person — for the chapter’s theme is the glorious raising of the dead in Christ. Each “all” is defined by its head.
23:
On 1 Corinthians 15:23 — “But every man in his own order: Christ the firstfruits; afterward they that are Christ’s at his coming” — the witnesses mark the order of the resurrection.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the military figure. Robertson: “order” (tagma), a military term — each in his own division or rank; “at his coming” (parousia), the technical word for a king’s arrival. Ellicott: the groups — Christ the firstfruits, then the faithful at his coming. The Reformed Calvin: God has appointed an order; we lie in the grave awaiting Christ’s coming.

The verse sets the resurrection in ordered sequence. Each in his own rank: Christ the firstfruits already risen; then those who are his, raised at his coming. The harvest follows the firstfruits in due order.

So the witnesses see Paul answer the question of timing by an appointed order. The witnesses note the military image in “order” — each raised in his own rank or division, not all at once but in God’s arranged sequence. First in the order stands Christ, the firstfruits, already risen; then, “afterward,” those who belong to him, raised at his “coming” — the parousia, the royal advent of the King. They mark Calvin’s point that this checks impatience: though Christ’s life draws ours after it, we do not rise immediately but await his appointed time, lying in the grave until his coming. They observe that the interval between the firstfruits and the harvest is not a denial of the resurrection but its orderly unfolding. The verse thus reassures the believer: the resurrection of Christ’s people is certain, secured by the firstfruits, and will come in its proper season at his glorious return. It also opens the sweep toward the end — Christ first, his people at his coming, and then, as the next verses show, the consummation when the kingdom is delivered to the Father.

So the witnesses see Paul answer the question of timing by an appointed order. The witnesses note the military image in “order” — each raised in his own rank, not all at once but in God’s arranged sequence. First stands Christ, the firstfruits, already risen; then those who belong to him, raised at his “coming,” the royal advent of the King. They mark Calvin’s point that this checks impatience: we await his appointed time. The interval between firstfruits and harvest is not a denial but the orderly unfolding of the resurrection, certain and secured.
24:
On 1 Corinthians 15:24 — “Then cometh the end, when he shall have delivered up the kingdom to God, even the Father; when he shall have put down all rule and all authority and power” — the witnesses mark the consummation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the end. Robertson: “the end” (telos), the consummation of the age; “deliver up” (paradido) the kingdom. Adam Clarke: “the mediatorial kingdom.” Ellicott: “all rule… good and bad” — the direct government of God at last attained. Wesley: “the end of the world… when he shall have abolished all adverse” power.

The verse looks to the final consummation. Then comes the end, when Christ delivers up the kingdom to the Father, having put down all rule and authority and power. The mediatorial reign reaches its goal.

So the witnesses see Paul carry the sequence to its ultimate term — “the end.” The witnesses note that after Christ and his people are raised comes the consummation of all things, when Christ delivers up the kingdom to God the Father. They mark that the kingdom here surrendered is the mediatorial kingdom — the administration committed to Christ for the redemption and government of his people — handed back when its purpose is complete; not that Christ ceases to reign as God, but that the special economy of mediation, having accomplished its end, is presented finished to the Father. They observe Ellicott’s note that “all rule and authority and power” — every intervening government, hostile and otherwise, made necessary by the fall — is put down, so that God’s direct reign is at last established. The verse thus sets the resurrection within the grand sweep of redemptive history: it is no mere individual hope but a stage toward the final triumph, when every opposing power is subdued and the kingdom is rendered up to the Father. It prepares the declaration that Christ must reign till all enemies are under his feet.

So the witnesses see Paul carry the sequence to its ultimate term — “the end.” The witnesses note that after Christ and his people are raised comes the consummation, when Christ delivers up the kingdom to the Father. They mark that the kingdom surrendered is the mediatorial kingdom — the administration committed to Christ for redemption — handed back when its purpose is complete; not that Christ ceases to reign as God. They observe Ellicott’s note that “all rule and authority and power” is put down, so that God’s direct reign is at last established. The resurrection is a stage toward the final triumph.
25:
On 1 Corinthians 15:25 — “For he must reign, till he hath put all enemies under his feet” — the witnesses mark the necessity of Christ’s reign.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the prophecy. Robertson: “till he put” (effective aorist), the goal of the reign. Clarke: from Psalm 110:1 — “sit thou at my right hand, until I make thine enemies thy footstool.” Ellicott: “a moral consequence” — God must triumph. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem warns against reading “till” to mean Christ shall cease to reign — “wrongly.”

The verse states why Christ reigns. He must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The kingdom continues until every foe is subdued.

So the witnesses see Paul ground Christ’s present reign in prophecy and purpose. The witnesses note the “must”: it is a moral necessity, founded on God’s decree and on Psalm 110, that Christ should reign until all his enemies are made his footstool. The reign continues to its appointed goal — the total subjugation of every opposing power. They mark the important caution Cyril of Jerusalem raises against a heretical misreading: the word “till” does not mean that Christ will cease to reign once his enemies are subdued — that would be a perverse inference; the “till” marks the certainty of the conquest, not the end of the kingship. They observe that the verse explains the “putting down” of verse 24: Christ’s mediatorial reign presses on until every foe — sin, Satan, and death — is vanquished. The verse thus assures the believer of the final, complete triumph of Christ: his reign is not in vain nor endless conflict, but a victorious progress toward the day when all enemies lie beneath his feet — the last of them, death itself, named in the verse following.

So the witnesses see Paul ground Christ’s present reign in prophecy and purpose. The witnesses note the “must”: a moral necessity, founded on God’s decree and Psalm 110, that Christ reign until all his enemies are his footstool. They mark the caution Cyril of Jerusalem raises against a heretical misreading: “till” does not mean Christ will cease to reign once his enemies are subdued; it marks the certainty of the conquest, not the end of the kingship. The verse explains the “putting down” of verse 24: the reign presses on until every foe — sin, Satan, and death — is vanquished.
26:
On 1 Corinthians 15:26 — “The last enemy that shall be destroyed is death” — the witnesses mark the final foe.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the order. Robertson: “death is done away, the last enemy” (futuristic present katargeitai). Adam Clarke: death “destroyed… by a general resurrection” — if none, it is not destroyed. Wesley: destroyed last, “after Satan… and sin… In the same order they prevailed” (Hebrews 2:14).

The verse names the last enemy. Death is the final foe to be destroyed. Its abolition, accomplished by the resurrection, is the crowning conquest of Christ’s reign.

So the witnesses see Paul name death as the last enemy Christ subdues. The witnesses note the climactic place of death in the order of conquest: Satan brought in sin, and sin brought forth death; and Christ, vanquishing them in turn, destroys death last of all. They mark the crucial point Clarke presses: death is destroyed not by mere cessation but by the resurrection — for so long as the dead remain in the grave, death still reigns; only the raising of the dead truly abolishes it. This binds the verse to the whole argument: the resurrection is necessary, for without it death is never destroyed and Christ’s reign never reaches its goal. They observe Wesley’s note on the order — Satan, sin, death — conquered in the reverse of how they prevailed. The verse thus shows the resurrection as the final victory: death, the last and most dreaded enemy, is at length done away when the dead are raised incorruptible. To deny the resurrection, then, is to leave death undefeated — an unthinkable failure of Christ’s reign, which must and shall abolish even the last enemy.

So the witnesses see Paul name death as the last enemy Christ subdues. The witnesses note its climactic place: Satan brought in sin, sin brought forth death; and Christ, vanquishing them in turn, destroys death last. They mark Clarke’s point that death is destroyed not by mere cessation but by the resurrection — so long as the dead remain in the grave, death still reigns; only the raising of the dead truly abolishes it. This binds the verse to the whole argument: without resurrection, death is never destroyed and Christ’s reign never reaches its goal.
27:
On 1 Corinthians 15:27 — “For he hath put all things under his feet. But when he saith all things are put under him, it is manifest that he is excepted, which did put all things under him” — the witnesses mark the one exception.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the citation and qualification. Robertson: God “put” (hypetaxen) all things under Christ (Psalm 8:6; cf. Hebrews 2:8); the Father is excepted. Adam Clarke: “the Father, who hath put all things under him… is excepted.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem: the Father “put all things in subjection” to the Son, yet is himself excepted.

The verse cites the promise and notes its limit. God has put all things under Christ’s feet; but plainly the Father, who subjected all things to him, is himself excepted. The Son’s universal dominion does not include the Father.

So the witnesses see Paul confirm Christ’s universal dominion from Scripture, with one necessary exception. The witnesses note the citation of Psalm 8: God has put all things under the feet of the man Christ Jesus — a comprehensive subjection of all creation to him. But Paul at once guards against a misreading: when Scripture says “all things” are put under him, it is self-evident that the Father, who is the one doing the subjecting, is not himself included among the things subjected. They mark that this careful qualification protects the truth of the Father’s supremacy in the economy of redemption, even as the Son reigns over all. They observe Cyril of Jerusalem’s use of the text to affirm the Son’s lordship over all things while distinguishing the Father who grants it. The verse thus establishes both the breadth of Christ’s dominion and its one exception, the Father — preparing the delicate statement of the next verse, that the Son himself will be subject to the Father, that God may be all in all, which Paul frames so as to guard the Son’s true deity even in his mediatorial subjection.

So the witnesses see Paul confirm Christ’s universal dominion from Scripture, with one necessary exception. The witnesses note the citation of Psalm 8: God has put all things under the feet of the man Christ Jesus. But Paul guards against a misreading: when Scripture says “all things” are put under him, it is self-evident that the Father, who does the subjecting, is not himself included. They mark that this careful qualification protects the Father’s supremacy in the economy of redemption, even as the Son reigns over all — preparing the delicate statement of the Son’s own subjection in the next verse.
28:
On 1 Corinthians 15:28 — “And when all things shall be subdued unto him, then shall the Son also himself be subject unto him that put all things under him, that God may be all in all” — the witnesses mark the final subjection and the goal.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the subjection. Adam Clarke: when the mediatorial kingdom closes, “the Son, as being man and Messiah,” is subject. Ellicott: “the complete redemption… the sublime conclusion.” Wesley: “the Son… shall deliver up the mediatorial kingdom… that the three-one God may be all in all.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem rebuts those who read it as the Son “absorbed into the Father.”

The verse states the consummation. When all is subdued, the Son himself is subject to the Father, that God may be all in all. The mediatorial work is rendered up, and God’s supremacy is complete.

So the witnesses see Paul reach the sublime conclusion: the Son subject, that God may be all in all. The witnesses are careful, with the expositors, to read the Son’s subjection rightly: it is not the subordination of his divine nature, nor an absorption into the Father, but the consummation of his mediatorial office — the Son, as the incarnate Messiah and head of the redeemed, presents the completed kingdom to the Father, his redemptive work accomplished. They mark Cyril of Jerusalem’s sharp rebuttal of those who twisted the verse to mean the Son is swallowed up or ceases to be — an impious misreading; the subjection concerns the economy of redemption, not the Son’s eternal deity. They observe the glorious goal: “that God may be all in all” — the whole redeemed creation brought into perfect, unmediated harmony with God, every enemy subdued, every creature in its place, God supreme and immediate in all. The verse thus crowns the great sweep from resurrection to consummation: through Christ’s victory, raised firstfruits and reigning King, all things are at last brought to God, who is all in all — the final end of redemption.

So the witnesses see Paul reach the sublime conclusion: the Son subject, that God may be all in all. The witnesses are careful to read the subjection rightly: not the subordination of his divine nature, nor an absorption into the Father, but the consummation of his mediatorial office — the Son presenting the completed kingdom to the Father. They mark Cyril of Jerusalem’s sharp rebuttal of those who twisted the verse to mean the Son is swallowed up — an impious misreading. They observe the glorious goal: the whole redeemed creation brought into perfect, unmediated harmony with God, every enemy subdued, God supreme in all.
29:
On 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?” — the witnesses mark one of the most disputed verses in the New Testament.

The Eastern fathers note the method. Chrysostom: Paul argues “from the very things which they practice,” bringing forward the gainsayers as witnesses by their own actions. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “this passage remains a puzzle” — over thirty interpretations. Adam Clarke: “the most difficult verse in the New Testament.” Lightfoot: baptism “in that sense as… death by martyrdom.” Wesley: “baptized in the room of the dead… like soldiers who advance.”

The verse returns to argument with a notoriously obscure question. If the dead rise not, what of those “baptized for the dead”? Why undergo it, if there is no resurrection? Paul presses the practice against the deniers.

So the witnesses see Paul resume his proof with a famously difficult argument from a practice. The witnesses freely acknowledge the verse’s obscurity — Clarke calls it the most difficult in the New Testament, and Robertson records over thirty proposed interpretations, no one certain. They note the common readings: some take “baptized for the dead” to mean baptism undergone in the prospect of death or martyrdom (Lightfoot), the believer professing faith though it may cost his life; others, “baptized in the room of the dead” (Wesley), new converts stepping into the ranks of those who have fallen, like soldiers advancing to fill the place of the slain; others still, a now-obscure custom at Corinth. They mark Chrysostom’s point about Paul’s method: he argues from the Corinthians’ own practice, whatever its exact form, to show that it presupposes the resurrection — for the practice would be pointless if the dead rise not. The verse thus, however interpreted, presses the same argument: the very actions of those who profess Christ assume a resurrection; to deny it empties their practice of meaning. Paul turns their own conduct into a witness against their error.

So the witnesses see Paul resume his proof with a famously difficult argument from a practice. The witnesses freely acknowledge the verse’s obscurity — Clarke calls it the most difficult in the New Testament, Robertson records over thirty interpretations. They note the common readings: baptism in prospect of death or martyrdom (Lightfoot); “in the room of the dead,” converts filling the ranks of the fallen (Wesley); or a now-obscure Corinthian custom. They mark Chrysostom’s point about Paul’s method: he argues from the Corinthians’ own practice, which presupposes the resurrection — turning their conduct into a witness against their error.
30:
"And why stand we in jeopardy every hour?" The brief verse notes Paul's appeal to his own constant peril as an argument for the resurrection. The grammatical voices note the question. Barnes and Robertson note Paul presses his argument from the apostles' sufferings: why do "we" (the apostles, the preachers) stand in jeopardy (danger, peril) every hour — constantly risking life — if there is no resurrection?

On the force, the witnesses note the implication. Barnes notes that the apostles' willingness to face perpetual danger would be irrational folly unless there is a future life and reward; their conduct proves their conviction of the resurrection.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: it would be madness to expose oneself to continual death for the gospel if death ended all; the apostles' hazards testify to their hope beyond the grave. The verse displays Paul's appeal to his own constant peril.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul presses his argument from the apostles' sufferings — why do they stand in jeopardy every hour, constantly risking life, if there is no resurrection? (Barnes, Robertson); their willingness to face perpetual danger being irrational unless there is a future life and reward, their conduct proving their conviction (Barnes). Calvin notes it would be madness to face continual death for the gospel if death ended all. The verse displays the appeal to peril. The verse marks the question: why in jeopardy? 31:
"I protest by your rejoicing which I have in Christ Jesus our Lord, I die daily." The witnesses note Paul's solemn affirmation that he dies daily. On "I protest by your rejoicing... in Christ Jesus," the grammatical voices note the oath. Barnes and Robertson note Paul makes a solemn protestation (an asseveration, nearly an oath) by "your rejoicing" — his glorying or boasting over them in Christ (his joy in them as his converts) — affirming the truth of what he says.

On "I die daily," the witnesses note the constant peril. Barnes and Calvin note Paul declares he "dies daily" — he is daily exposed to death, lives in constant danger and self-denial, dying to self and risking his life continually for the gospel.

The Reformed Calvin notes the force: this daily dying confirms the previous argument — Paul's whole life of perpetual peril would be senseless without the hope of resurrection. The verse displays Paul's solemn affirmation that he dies daily.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul makes a solemn protestation (nearly an oath) by "your rejoicing" — his glorying over them in Christ, his joy in his converts — affirming the truth of what he says (Barnes, Robertson); and declares he "dies daily," daily exposed to death, living in constant danger and self-denial for the gospel (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes this confirms the argument: such a life of peril would be senseless without resurrection hope. The verse displays Paul's daily dying. The verse marks the oath: I protest. 32:
"If after the manner of men I have fought with beasts at Ephesus, what advantageth it me, if the dead rise not? let us eat and drink; for to morrow we die." The witnesses note Paul's peril at Ephesus and the folly of suffering without resurrection — and the beasts crux. On "I have fought with beasts at Ephesus," the great debate divides. The grammatical-historical Lightfoot and Barnes lean to a literal sense — Paul was actually condemned to fight wild beasts in the arena, a real and deadly peril (though not recorded in Acts). The grammatical-historical Ellicott, Robertson (cautiously), and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley take it figuratively — "beasts" being fierce, savage men (the Ephesian opponents), since a Roman citizen would not lawfully be cast to beasts and the event is absent from Acts and the peril-list of 2 Corinthians 11.

On "if the dead rise not, let us eat and drink; for tomorrow we die," the witnesses note the Epicurean maxim. Barnes and Calvin note Paul quotes the saying (from Isaiah 22:13, the cry of the abandoned) — if there is no resurrection, the only consistent course is the Epicurean one: indulge in present pleasure, for death ends all.

The witnesses note the argument: Paul's sufferings would be pure folly, and pleasure-seeking the logical course, if there were no resurrection. The verse displays Paul's peril at Ephesus and the folly of suffering without resurrection.

The witnesses agree the argument: Paul's deadly peril at Ephesus would be senseless, and the Epicurean "let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die" (from Isaiah 22:13) the only consistent course, if the dead rise not (Barnes, Calvin). But "fought with beasts" divides them: Lightfoot and Barnes lean literal (Paul actually condemned to the arena, a real peril absent from Acts); Ellicott, Robertson (cautiously), Clarke, and Wesley take it figuratively of fierce men, since a Roman citizen would not lawfully be cast to beasts and the event is unrecorded. The verse displays the peril and the folly without resurrection. The verse marks the peril: beasts at Ephesus. The verse displays the debate: literal beasts or fierce men. The verse displays the senselessness of Christian suffering apart from the resurrection, and warns against the corrupting company that denies it (vv.33-34).
33:
"Be not deceived: evil communications corrupt good manners." The brief verse notes the warning that bad company corrupts good character. The grammatical voices note the proverb. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes a line from the Greek poet Menander (a current proverb): "evil communications (bad company, corrupt associations) corrupt good manners (good morals, character)" — warning that fellowship with those who deny the resurrection will undermine sound faith and life.

On "be not deceived," the witnesses note the caution. Barnes notes Paul warns them not to be led astray: the error of the resurrection-deniers is not harmless speculation but a corrupting influence on morals and faith.

The Reformed Calvin notes the application: false doctrine, especially that which removes the hope of the resurrection and judgment, breeds loose living (as the Epicurean maxim shows); the company of such errorists is to be shunned. The verse displays the warning that bad company corrupts good character.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes a line from Menander (a current proverb) — "evil communications (bad company) corrupt good manners (good morals)" — warning that fellowship with resurrection-deniers will undermine sound faith and life (Barnes, Robertson); "be not deceived" cautioning that their error is no harmless speculation but a corrupting influence (Barnes). Calvin notes false doctrine that removes the hope of resurrection breeds loose living, so such company is to be shunned. The verse displays the warning against bad company. 34:
"Awake to righteousness, and sin not; for some have not the knowledge of God: I speak this to your shame." The witnesses note the call to awake to righteousness and the rebuke of ignorance. On "awake to righteousness, and sin not," the grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul calls them to "awake" (as from a drunken stupor or sleep) "to righteousness" — to rouse themselves to sober, holy living, and to "sin not," abandoning the error and loose conduct.

On "some have not the knowledge of God," the witnesses note the rebuke. Barnes notes Paul charges that "some" among them are in deep ignorance of God — not truly knowing him, whatever their pretensions; the denial of the resurrection betrays a fundamental ignorance of God's power and character.

On "I speak this to your shame," the Reformed Calvin notes the sharp reproof: it is to their shame that such ignorance and error should exist among them, a church so endowed with gifts; Paul rebukes them to rouse them. The verse displays the call to awake to righteousness and the rebuke of ignorance.

The thin comment matches the verse. 35:
"But some man will say, How are the dead raised up? and with what body do they come?" The witnesses note the objection: how and with what body are the dead raised? On "how are the dead raised up?" the grammatical voices note the first question. Barnes and Robertson note Paul anticipates the objector (perhaps a scoffer, or one perplexed): "how" — by what means, what process — can the dead, long decayed, be raised at all? The seeming impossibility was the great obstacle.

On "with what body do they come?" the witnesses note the second question. Barnes and Chrysostom note the further difficulty: "with what body" — what kind of body will the risen have, since the old body has perished and dissolved? The objection presses both the possibility and the nature of the resurrection body.

The eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the answer to come: Paul will meet the objection not by philosophical proof but by analogies from nature (the seed) and by revelation, showing that God can and will give a fitting body. The verse displays the objection: how and with what body are the dead raised?

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul anticipates the objector — "how" (by what means) can the dead, long decayed, be raised at all? the seeming impossibility being the great obstacle (Barnes, Robertson); and "with what body" — what kind of body will the risen have, since the old has perished? (Barnes, Chrysostom). Chrysostom and Calvin note Paul will answer not by philosophy but by analogies from nature (the seed) and by revelation. The verse displays the objection.
