36:
"Thou fool, that which thou sowest is not quickened, except it die:" The witnesses note the analogy of the seed that must die to live. On "thou fool," the grammatical voices note the rebuke. Barnes and Robertson note Paul answers the objector sharply: "thou fool" (senseless one) — the objection betrays folly, for the very analogy of nature, daily before his eyes, answers it.

On "that which thou sowest is not quickened, except it die," the witnesses note the analogy. Barnes and Calvin note the seed sown in the ground must first "die" (decay, dissolve, lose its old form) before it is "quickened" (made alive, springs up into new life); death is the very gateway to the new life — so it is no absurdity that the body must die to be raised.

The Reformed Calvin notes the force: nature itself, in the dying and rising of the seed, refutes the objector; God's power to bring life out of death is daily displayed in the field. The verse displays the analogy of the seed that must die to live.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul answers sharply — "thou fool," the objection betraying folly, for the analogy of nature daily before the objector's eyes answers it (Barnes, Robertson); the seed sown must first "die" (decay, lose its old form) before it is "quickened" (made alive, springs up), death being the gateway to new life, so it is no absurdity that the body must die to be raised (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes nature itself, in the dying and rising seed, refutes the objector. The verse displays the seed analogy. The verse marks the rebuke: thou fool. The verse displays the analogy: the seed must die. The verse displays the principle: death the gateway to life. The verse displays the force: nature refutes the objection. 37:
"And that which thou sowest, thou sowest not that body that shall be, but bare grain, it may chance of wheat, or of some other grain:" The brief verse notes that the seed sown differs from the body that springs up. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note Paul develops the analogy: what is sown is not "that body that shall be" (the full plant with stalk and ear) but "bare grain" — a mere naked seed, of wheat or some other kind; the body that rises from it is far different from what was sown.

On the application, the witnesses note the lesson. Barnes notes the point: the risen body need not be identical in form or qualities to the body that was buried; as the plant differs gloriously from the bare seed, so the resurrection body will differ from the body laid in the grave.

The Reformed Calvin notes the comfort: the objector's difficulty (the dissolution of the body) is no obstacle, for God brings a glorious body from the buried seed, far surpassing what was sown. The verse displays that the seed sown differs from the body that springs up.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul develops the analogy — what is sown is not "that body that shall be" (the full plant) but "bare grain," a naked seed of wheat or some other kind, the body that rises far different from what was sown (Barnes, Robertson); so the risen body need not be identical in form to the buried, as the plant differs gloriously from the seed (Barnes). Calvin notes the dissolution of the body is no obstacle, God bringing a glorious body from the buried seed. The verse displays the seed differing from the plant. The verse marks the contrast: bare grain, not the plant. The verse displays the lesson: the risen body differs. The verse displays the comfort: a glorious body from the seed. 38:
"But God giveth it a body as it hath pleased him, and to every seed his own body." The brief verse notes that God gives each seed its own fitting body. The grammatical voices note the divine agency. Barnes and Robertson note Paul ascribes the whole to God: "God giveth it a body as it hath pleased him" — the transformation of seed into plant is God's own work, according to his sovereign pleasure and wisdom.

On "to every seed his own body," the witnesses note the order. Barnes notes that God gives "to every seed his own body" — each kind of seed produces its own appropriate plant (wheat from wheat, etc.); there is order and fitness, each receiving the body suited to it.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the application: as God, by his power and wisdom, clothes each seed with its fitting body, so he can and will give the risen dead a fitting resurrection body — the difficulty of "with what body?" answered by God's creative power. The verse displays that God gives each seed its own fitting body.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul ascribes the whole to God — "God giveth it a body as it hath pleased him," the transformation of seed into plant being God's own work according to his sovereign pleasure (Barnes, Robertson); and "to every seed his own body," each kind producing its own appropriate plant, order and fitness in all (Barnes). Wesley notes that as God clothes each seed with its fitting body, so he will give the risen dead a fitting resurrection body. The verse displays God giving each its body. The verse marks the agency: God giveth. The verse displays the pleasure: as it pleased him. The verse displays the order: each its own body. The verse displays the application: God will give the risen body. The giving displays God's power and wisdom in the resurrection. The verse displays the giving: by God. 39:
"All flesh is not the same flesh: but there is one kind of flesh of men, another flesh of beasts, another of fishes, and another of birds." The brief verse notes the variety of flesh among creatures. The grammatical voices note the observation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul shows the variety in God's creation: "all flesh is not the same" — there are different kinds of flesh, of men, of beasts, of fishes, of birds, each suited to its creature and element.

On the inference, the witnesses note the point. Barnes notes that since God has made so many kinds of flesh, each fitted to its purpose, it is no difficulty for him to make a new kind of body — a resurrection body — different from the present.

The Reformed Calvin notes the application: the manifold variety of bodies in creation proves God's power and freedom to give the risen a body of a different and higher kind. The verse displays the variety of flesh among creatures.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul shows the variety in God's creation — "all flesh is not the same," there being different kinds of flesh, of men, beasts, fishes, birds, each suited to its creature (Barnes, Robertson); so since God has made so many kinds, it is no difficulty for him to make a new kind of body for the resurrection (Barnes). Calvin notes the manifold variety proves God's power and freedom to give the risen a higher kind of body. The verse displays the variety of flesh. 40:
"There are also celestial bodies, and bodies terrestrial: but the glory of the celestial is one, and the glory of the terrestrial is another." The brief verse notes the differing glory of heavenly and earthly bodies. The grammatical voices note the distinction. Barnes and Robertson note Paul extends the argument to "celestial bodies" (the heavenly — sun, moon, stars) and "bodies terrestrial" (the earthly — men, animals, plants); these are of different orders, with different kinds of glory.

On "the glory of the celestial is one, of the terrestrial another," the witnesses note the contrast of glories. Barnes notes that the heavenly bodies have one kind of glory (splendour, brightness) and the earthly another; God adorns each order with its own appropriate glory.

The grammatical-historical note the inference: as God has clothed earthly and heavenly bodies each with its own glory, so he can clothe the resurrection body with a new and higher glory than the present. The verse displays the differing glory of heavenly and earthly bodies.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul extends the argument to "celestial bodies" (sun, moon, stars) and "bodies terrestrial" (earthly creatures), of different orders with different glory (Barnes, Robertson); the heavenly having one kind of glory and the earthly another, God adorning each with its own appropriate glory (Barnes). 41:
"There is one glory of the sun, and another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars: for one star differeth from another star in glory." The brief verse notes the differing glories even among the heavenly lights. The grammatical voices note the gradation. 42:
"So also is the resurrection of the dead. It is sown in corruption; it is raised in incorruption:" The witnesses note the resurrection: sown in corruption, raised in incorruption. On "so also is the resurrection of the dead," the grammatical voices note the application. Barnes and Robertson note Paul now applies all the analogies (seed, flesh, glory) directly: "so also" is the resurrection — the same principle of transformation and difference governs the rising of the dead.

Barnes and Calvin note the body is "sown" (buried) "in corruption" — subject to decay, dissolution, putrefaction — and "raised in incorruption" — imperishable, no longer liable to decay or death; the corruptible exchanged for the incorruptible.

The Reformed Calvin notes the identity-with-change: it is the same body that is sown and raised, but raised in a transformed, incorruptible state; not a different body but the same gloriously changed. The verse displays the resurrection: sown in corruption, raised in incorruption.

The thin comment matches the verse. 43:
"It is sown in dishonour; it is raised in glory: it is sown in weakness; it is raised in power:" The brief verse notes the body raised in glory and power. The grammatical voices note the two further contrasts. Barnes and Robertson note the body is "sown in dishonour" (the corpse, an object of corruption, laid away with grief) and "raised in glory" (radiant, honoured, glorified); and "sown in weakness" (helpless in death, powerless) and "raised in power" (vigorous, strong, no longer subject to weakness).

On the transformation, the witnesses note the elevation. Barnes notes the resurrection body, far from the corruption, dishonour, and weakness of the buried corpse, will be glorious and powerful — fitted for the heavenly state.

44:
"It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body. There is a natural body, and there is a spiritual body." The witnesses note the natural body raised a spiritual body — and the crux of its nature. On "sown a natural body, raised a spiritual body," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the body is "sown a natural (psychikon, animal) body" — endowed with animal life, sustained by breath, food, sleep, subject to the vital functions — and "raised a spiritual (pneumatikon) body" — not a mere spirit, but a body so governed by the Spirit as to be free of the animal functions, sustained as we conceive a spirit to be.

On the nature of the change, a key point divides interpreters. The Reformed Calvin emphatically holds that the SUBSTANCE of the body remains the same; only its QUALITY changes — there is no mention of a change of substance, against those who imagine the body's substance becomes spirit. Barnes likewise denies that "spiritual" means a refined or sublimated substance — it means a body without the vital functions, sustained like a spirit; the identity of the body is preserved.

"Therefore, my beloved brethren, be ye stedfast, unmoveable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, forasmuch as ye know that your labour is not in vain in the Lord." The witnesses note the practical conclusion of the resurrection doctrine.
